comments.body: Could you reduce the sample code to the essence? We don't need the "which week of month" code and it confused me at first glance.

For new readers to this question stay far away from the old and obsolete classes Calendar and SimpleDateFormat. Since Java 8 use java.time, the modern Java date and time API. See the good answer by Basil Bourque. The old classes were troublesome and cumbersome to work with, simply don’t try.
I did. Still the same result. 7 days ahead from the passed day.

answers.body:
You will need to use this line of code somewhere in you current code:

calendar.set(Calendar.DAY_OF_WEEK, Calendar.MONDAY);

it should be at least before the for-loop.

you need to set the day after you set the date

calendar.set(mYear, mMonth, Integer.parseInt(extractedDay)); calendar.set(Calendar.DAY_OF_WEEK, Calendar.MONDAY);

comments.body: I've just tryed other days of this week. It output same results. What date are you trying?
16 May. I try via now.set(2012, 4, 16) or any day other than Thu
I get this output:[05/14/2012, 05/15/2012, 05/16/2012, 05/17/2012, 05/18/2012, 05/19/2012, 05/20/2012] If I correctly understood your question, it is correct. Your output is different?
Hm, my code is slightly different. But its core is yours. Let me see which part is making a mistake. Will get back...

I added additional lines and messed up you code. Your code like this works fine. Except that it does not generate the right week when I select Sunday, but I do not care for that as Sunday will never be passed as a parameter.
today is sunday, and it skipped current date and start from tomorrow

answers.body:
You have first to subtract the weekday of the day you choose, in order to start from the first day of week. Try the following code:

public static void main(String[] args){
    Calendar now = Calendar.getInstance();

    SimpleDateFormat format = new SimpleDateFormat("MM/dd/yyyy");

    String[] days = new String[7];
    int delta = -now.get(GregorianCalendar.DAY_OF_WEEK) + 2; //add 2 if your week start on monday
    now.add(Calendar.DAY_OF_MONTH, delta );
    for (int i = 0; i < 7; i++)
    {
        days[i] = format.format(now.getTime());
        now.add(Calendar.DAY_OF_MONTH, 1);
    }
    System.out.println(Arrays.toString(days));

}

Today, it output:

[04/30/2012, 05/01/2012, 05/02/2012, 05/03/2012, 05/04/2012, 05/05/2012, 05/06/2012]

Your question has already been answered, but I'd like to add a bit. Your code is problematic in two ways:

  1. It uses various data types for the initial date (mYear is int and exactDay is String?).
  2. It mixes parsing, formatting and generation of the result.

That makes it hard for you to test the code!

Therefore, I propose a different approach:

public class DaysOfCurrentWeek {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Date refDate = new Date();

        Date[] days = getDaysOfWeek(refDate, Calendar.getInstance().getFirstDayOfWeek());

        for (Date day : days) {
            System.out.println(day);
        }
    }

    private static Date[] getDaysOfWeek(Date refDate, int firstDayOfWeek) {
        Calendar calendar = Calendar.getInstance();
        calendar.setTime(refDate);
        calendar.set(Calendar.DAY_OF_WEEK, firstDayOfWeek);
        Date[] daysOfWeek = new Date[7];
        for (int i = 0; i < 7; i++) {
            daysOfWeek[i] = calendar.getTime();
            calendar.add(Calendar.DAY_OF_MONTH, 1);
        }
        return daysOfWeek;
    }
}

The code works like this:

  1. Create a reference date (in this example just by using new Date()).
  2. Call getDaysOfWeek() to do the "dirty job". By passing firstDayOfWeek you are free to choose MONDAY or SUNDAY as start of the week. In this example, I just use the default of the calendar.
  3. Return the result - unformatted, and as Date. This way you can let the calling code decide what to do - print it or do some further calculations.

Now you can simply test the getDaysOfWeek() method without worrying about formatting or how the reference date has been created.

Modern Java

In Java 8+, use only java.time classes for your date-time handling. Never use Date, Calendar, SimpleDateFormat, etc.

I pass May 2 and I get an array [30,1,2,3,4,5,6] which is this week actually. Or, I pass May 16 and I get an array [14,15,16,17,18,19,20].

Today

To represent a date-only value (year-month-day), use java.time.LocalDate class.

Determining today’s date requires a time zone. For any given moment, the time and the date vary around the globe by time zone. While “tomorrow” in Tokyo Japan it is simultaneously “yesterday” in Toledo Ohio US. If omitted, the JVM’s current default time zone is applied implicitly.

ZoneId z = ZoneId.of( "America/Edmonton" ) ;
LocalDate today = LocalDate.now( z ) ;

Week

Move to the date for the day-of-week you consider to be the first day of the week. We use a TemporalAdjuster from the utility class TemporalAdjusters (plural).

LocalDate firstOfWeek = today.with( TemporalAdjusters.previousOrSame( DayOfWeek.MONDAY ) ) ;
LocalDate lastOfWeek = firstOfWeek.plusWeeks( 1 ) ;

To get each date in that range, get a stream of LocalDate objects.

List< LocalDate > datesOfWeek = firstOfWeek.datesUntil( lastOfWeek ).toList() ;

But you actually want an array of the day number of each date. You used a String array but int array makes more sense. To produce this, transform our stream of date objects into an IntStream of day numbers.

int[] datesOfWeek =
        firstOfWeek
                .datesUntil( lastOfWeek )                // Stream < LocalDate >
                .mapToInt( LocalDate :: getDayOfMonth )  // IntStream
                .toArray();                              // int[]

Full example code

ZoneId z = ZoneId.of( "America/Edmonton" );
LocalDate today = LocalDate.now( z );
LocalDate firstOfWeek = today.with( TemporalAdjusters.previousOrSame( DayOfWeek.MONDAY ) );
LocalDate lastOfWeek = firstOfWeek.plusWeeks( 1 );
int[] datesOfWeek = firstOfWeek.datesUntil( lastOfWeek ).mapToInt( LocalDate :: getDayOfMonth ).toArray();

IO.println( java.util.Arrays.toString( datesOfWeek ) );  // In earlier Java, swap `System.out` for `IO`.

[17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23]

title: Get all days of a current week?
body:
I need to get numbers of all days of a week (MON to SUN) based on which day is sent as a parameter.

For example,
I pass May 2 and I get an array [30,1,2,3,4,5,6] which is this week actually.
Or, I pass May 16 and I get an array [14,15,16,17,18,19,20].

I tried to use this code, but it return 7 days from today, which I do not want. 

        //get which week of month
        Calendar now = Calendar.getInstance();
        now.set(mYear, mMonth, Integer.parseInt(extractedDay));
        int weekOfMonth = now.get(Calendar.WEEK_OF_MONTH);

        //get all days of clicked week
        SimpleDateFormat format = new SimpleDateFormat("MM/dd/yyyy");
        Calendar calendar = Calendar.getInstance();
        calendar.setFirstDayOfWeek(Calendar.MONDAY);
        calendar.set(mYear, mMonth, Integer.parseInt(extractedDay));

        String[] days = new String[7];
        for (int i = 0; i < 7; i++)
        {
            days[i] = format.format(calendar.getTime());
            calendar.add(Calendar.DAY_OF_MONTH, 1);
        }

But this is not what I need. I need to pass today's day (THU) and to get dates of this week of May - Mon, Apr 30 to Sun, May 6.

EDIT

I realized that I have a code which detects which week of a month it is. Is there a way to use this data and to set a date for Monday? For example, I pass May 16 and then I detect that it's 3rd week of May, and the I set the variable firstDayOfWeek (a new variable) to May 14.
